title: ElasticNet: The Hybrid
concept: ElasticNet combines L1 and L2 penalties, providing both weight shrinkage and feature selection simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference in weight behavior between Ridge (L2) and Lasso (L1)?

options.text: Ridge shrinks weights close to zero; Lasso pushes them to exactly zero
Ridge multiplies weights; Lasso divides weights
Ridge only works on numbers; Lasso only works on text labels

exercises.explain: Correct! Ridge (L2) keeps all features but shrinks weights; Lasso (L1) can completely drop features by setting weights to zero.

cards.title: Best of both worlds
cards.body: Imagine you're trying to choose between a fuel-efficient hybrid car and a high-power sports car. Why choose? A hybrid sports car combines both to give you efficiency when cruising and speed when accelerating.

cards.title: What is ElasticNet?
cards.body: ElasticNet is a regularization technique that combines both L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) penalties into a single cost function, balanced by a tuning parameter.

cards.title: Two knobs
cards.body: Think of ElasticNet like a sound mixing board with two volume sliders: one slider controls L1 (to mute useless channels) and the other controls L2 (to smoothly balance the volumes of the remaining channels).

cards.title: Correlated features
cards.body: If you predict home value using square footage, number of rooms, and number of windows (which are highly correlated), Lasso might randomly pick one and delete the rest. ElasticNet will group them and keep them together.

cards.title: Not twice the penalty
cards.body: A common myth is that ElasticNet just doubles the penalties. Actually, it uses a mixing ratio (often called l1_ratio) to decide how much L1 vs. L2 to apply, letting you customize the balance.

cards.title: Comparing three regularizers
cards.body: Ridge, Lasso, and ElasticNet visual comparison:

left.text:
Lasso: Only feature selection.
Ridge: Only weight shrinkage.
Strictly separate behaviors.

right.text:
Performs feature selection.
Retains group correlations.
Flexible hybrid approach.

exercises.prompt: What does ElasticNet combine in its penalty function?

options.text: Both linear regression and decision trees
Both gradient descent and random forests

exercises.explain: ElasticNet combines L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) penalties to leverage the strengths of both.

exercises.prompt: True or false: ElasticNet is particularly useful when features in the dataset are highly correlated.
exercises.explain: True! While Lasso randomly throws away correlated features, ElasticNet can keep them grouped and shrink them together.

exercises.sentence: ElasticNet uses a mixing ratio parameter to adjust the balance between ___ and L2 penalties.
exercises.explain: The ratio determines the balance between L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) regularization.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following equations represents ElasticNet's penalty term?
exercises.explain: ElasticNet adds both the absolute weight penalty (L1) and the squared weight penalty (L2) together.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to predict retail sales using 100 features. Several features (like household income, local GDP, and average spending) are highly correlated. You want feature selection, but you don't want the model to randomly delete these correlated variables. What should you do?

options.text: Use ElasticNet, because it selects features but handles correlated groups better than Lasso.
Use pure Lasso, because random deletion is acceptable.
Avoid all regularization to preserve correlations.

exercises.explain: Brilliant! ElasticNet's L2 component helps it select groups of correlated features together, while its L1 component still drives useless features to zero. Next, let's learn how to choose the right regularizer for any dataset!
